original_abstract: Widespread groundwater contamination by per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) necessitates efficient removal strategies during drinking water treatment, particularly for increasingly prevalent short-chain compounds. This study evaluates the comparative performance of granular activated carbon (GAC) and various anion exchange (IEX) resins (both strong and weak base) through laboratory batch experiments and pilot-scale column tests at a PFAS-polluted waterworks. Results demonstrate that sorption affinity is strongly dependent on chain length and functional groups; for all materials, short-chain perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCA), such as PFBA and PFPeA, exhibited significantly lower affinities compared to long-chain analogs or short-chain sulfonic acids (PFSA). In column tests, GAC achieved sufficient removal of PFBA and PFPeA only over limited run times (6,000 and 11,000 bed volumes, respectively), whereas longer PFCA (C≥6) allowed for extended operation. While IEX media provided higher capacities than GAC for certain short-chain compounds, their higher material costs make them economically uncompetitive in single-use mode. However, weak base ion exchangers demonstrated the potential for circular operation through regeneration. While long-chain PFAS required ethanol-based mixtures for effective elution, short-chain compounds like PFBA could be recovered using standard aqueous regenerants. These findings suggest that IEX with regeneration and subsequent liquid-liquid separation offers a viable economic and technical alternative to GAC for managing complex PFAS profiles in drinking water.
humanized_abstract: The increasing number of discoveries of groundwater contaminated with per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) necessitates development of effective PFAS removal processes for drinking water treatment, especially in response to emerging short-chain compounds. In this study, the performance of granular activated carbon (GAC) and various anion exchange resins, including strong and weak base IEX resins, for PFAS removal was evaluated through laboratory batch experiments and pilot-scale column experiments at a PFAS-contaminated waterworks. Results show that the relative extent of PFAS sorption to these media was heavily dependent on the specific PFAS, with generally shorter-chain perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCA) having lower affinity to all media evaluated compared with longer-chain PFCAs as well as shorter-chain sulfonic acids (PFSAs). In the column experiments, the GAC had relatively short breakthrough curves corresponding to only about 6,000 and 11,000 bed volumes of PFBA and PFPeA, respectively, while longer-chain PFCAs had much longer service times. The IEX media had higher capacity for removal of the shorter-chain PFAS than GAC, although their higher cost when used as single-use media precludes their use as an economical alternative. However, the weak base IEX media exhibited the potential for circular use due to regeneration of the medium. While long-chain PFAS required ethanol solution for elution, short-chain PFAS such as PFBA can be eluted by aqueous regeneration solution. Overall, the IEX process with regeneration coupled with liquid-liquid separation was found to have an economical and technical advantage for removal of PFAS contaminants having a complex composition that contains a wide distribution of chain lengths.

In the column experiments, the GAC had relatively short breakthrough curves corresponding to only about 6,000 and 11,000 bed volumes of PFBA and PFPeA, respectively, while longer-chain PFCAs had much longer service times.
